question:
Suppose that $f: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ has a continuous derivative
and that $\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx = 0$. Prove that for every $\alpha \in (0,1)$,
\[
\left| \int_0^\alpha f(x)\,dx \right| \leq \frac{1}{8} \max_{0 \leq x
\leq 1} |f'(x)|.
\]
solution:
Put $B = \max_{0 \leq x \leq 1} |f'(x)|$
and $g(x) = \int_0^x f(y)\,dy$. Since $g(0) = g(1) = 0$, the maximum value
of $|g(x)|$ must occur at a critical point $y \in (0,1)$ satisfying
$g'(y) = f(y) = 0$. We may thus take $\alpha = y$ hereafter.

Since $\int_0^\alpha f(x)\,dx = -\int_0^{1-\alpha} f(1-x)\,dx$,
we may assume that $\alpha \leq 1/2$. By then substituting $-f(x)$
for $f(x)$ if needed, we may assume that $\int_0^\alpha f(x)\,dx \geq 0$.
{}From the inequality $f'(x) \geq -B$, we deduce
$f(x) \leq B(\alpha - x)$ for $0 \leq x \leq \alpha$, so
\begin{align*}
\int_0^\alpha f(x)\,dx \leq &\int_0^\alpha B(\alpha-x)\,dx \\
&= -\left. \frac{1}{2} B (\alpha - x)^2 \right|_0^\alpha \\
&= \frac{\alpha^2}{2} B \leq \frac{1}{8} B
\end{align*}
as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Suppose that $realfunc: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ has a continuous derivative
and that \(\int_0^1 realfunc(absciss)\,d absciss = 0\). Prove that for every $fraction \in (0,1)$,
\[
\left| \int_0^{fraction} realfunc(absciss)\,d absciss \right| \leq \frac{1}{8} \max_{0 \leq absciss
\leq 1} |realfunc'(absciss)|.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Put $maxbound = \max_{0 \leq absciss \leq 1} |realfunc'(absciss)|$
and $primitive(absciss) = \int_0^{absciss} realfunc(ordinate)\,d ordinate$. Since $primitive(0) = primitive(1) = 0$, the maximum value
of $|primitive(absciss)|$ must occur at a critical point $ordinate \in (0,1)$ satisfying
$primitive'(ordinate) = realfunc(ordinate) = 0$. We may thus take $fraction = ordinate$ hereafter.

Since $\int_0^{fraction} realfunc(absciss)\,d absciss = -\int_0^{1-fraction} realfunc(1-absciss)\,d absciss$,
we may assume that $fraction \leq 1/2$. By then substituting $-realfunc(absciss)$
for $realfunc(absciss)$ if needed, we may assume that $\int_0^{fraction} realfunc(absciss)\,d absciss \geq 0$.
From the inequality $realfunc'(absciss) \geq -maxbound$, we deduce
$realfunc(absciss) \leq maxbound(fraction - absciss)$ for $0 \leq absciss \leq fraction$, so
\begin{align*}
\int_0^{fraction} realfunc(absciss)\,d absciss &\leq \int_0^{fraction} maxbound(fraction-absciss)\,d absciss \\
&= -\left. \frac{1}{2} maxbound (fraction - absciss)^2 \right|_0^{fraction} \\
&= \frac{fraction^2}{2} maxbound \leq \frac{1}{8} maxbound
\end{align*}
as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that $newspaper: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ has a continuous derivative
and that $\int_0^1 newspaper(chandelier)\,dchandelier = 0$. Prove that for every $rainstorm \in (0,1)$,
\[
\left| \int_0^{rainstorm} newspaper(chandelier)\,dchandelier \right| \leq \frac{1}{8} \max_{0 \leq chandelier
\leq 1} |newspaper'(chandelier)|.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Put $toothpick = \max_{0 \leq chandelier \leq 1} |newspaper'(chandelier)|$
and $marshmallow(chandelier) = \int_0^{chandelier} newspaper(hurricane)\,dhurricane$. Since $marshmallow(0) = marshmallow(1) = 0$, the maximum value
of $|marshmallow(chandelier)|$ must occur at a critical point $hurricane \in (0,1)$ satisfying
$marshmallow'(hurricane) = newspaper(hurricane) = 0$. We may thus take $rainstorm = hurricane$ hereafter.

Since $\int_0^{rainstorm} newspaper(chandelier)\,dchandelier = -\int_0^{1-rainstorm} newspaper(1-chandelier)\,dchandelier$,
we may assume that $rainstorm \leq 1/2$. By then substituting $-newspaper(chandelier)$
for $newspaper(chandelier)$ if needed, we may assume that $\int_0^{rainstorm} newspaper(chandelier)\,dchandelier \geq 0$.
{}From the inequality $newspaper'(chandelier) \geq -toothpick$, we deduce
$newspaper(chandelier) \leq toothpick(rainstorm - chandelier)$ for $0 \leq chandelier \leq rainstorm$, so
\begin{align*}
\int_0^{rainstorm} newspaper(chandelier)\,dchandelier \leq &\int_0^{rainstorm} toothpick(rainstorm-chandelier)\,dchandelier \\
&= -\left. \frac{1}{2} toothpick (rainstorm - chandelier)^2 \right|_0^{rainstorm} \\
&= \frac{rainstorm^2}{2} toothpick \leq \frac{1}{8} toothpick
\end{align*}
as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that $nonvarying: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ has a continuous derivative
and that $\int_0^1 nonvarying(fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint = 0$. Prove that for every $infinity \in (0,1)$,
\[
\left| \int_0^{infinity} nonvarying(fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint \right| \leq \frac{1}{8} \max_{0 \leq fixedpoint
\leq 1} |nonvarying'(fixedpoint)|.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Put $lowestval = \max_{0 \leq fixedpoint \leq 1} |nonvarying'(fixedpoint)|$
and $discrete(fixedpoint) = \int_0^{fixedpoint} nonvarying(constant)\,d constant$. Since $discrete(0) = discrete(1) = 0$, the maximum value
of $|discrete(fixedpoint)|$ must occur at a critical point $constant \in (0,1)$ satisfying
$discrete'(constant) = nonvarying(constant) = 0$. We may thus take $infinity = constant$ hereafter.

Since $\int_0^{infinity} nonvarying(fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint = -\int_0^{1-infinity} nonvarying(1-fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint$,
we may assume that $infinity \leq 1/2$. By then substituting $-nonvarying(fixedpoint)$
for $nonvarying(fixedpoint)$ if needed, we may assume that $\int_0^{infinity} nonvarying(fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint \geq 0$.
{}From the inequality $nonvarying'(fixedpoint) \geq -lowestval$, we deduce
$nonvarying(fixedpoint) \leq lowestval(infinity - fixedpoint)$ for $0 \leq fixedpoint \leq infinity$, so
\begin{align*}
\int_0^{infinity} nonvarying(fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint \leq &\int_0^{infinity} lowestval(infinity-fixedpoint)\,d fixedpoint \\
&= -\left. \frac{1}{2} lowestval (infinity - fixedpoint)^2 \right|_0^{infinity} \\
&= \frac{infinity^2}{2} lowestval \leq \frac{1}{8} lowestval
\end{align*}
as desired.

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that $qofuihgs: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ has a continuous derivative
and that $\int_0^1 qofuihgs(zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt = 0$. Prove that for every $vpsgnkmu \in (0,1)$,
\[
\left| \int_0^{vpsgnkmu} qofuihgs(zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt \right| \leq \frac{1}{8} \max_{0 \leq zemyofqt
\leq 1} |qofuihgs'(zemyofqt)|.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Put $moypqksa = \max_{0 \leq zemyofqt \leq 1} |qofuihgs'(zemyofqt)|$
and $twlzrnka(zemyofqt) = \int_0^{zemyofqt} qofuihgs(hleruvkc)\,dhleruvkc$. Since $twlzrnka(0) = twlzrnka(1) = 0$, the maximum value
of $|twlzrnka(zemyofqt)|$ must occur at a critical point $hleruvkc \in (0,1)$ satisfying
$twlzrnka'(hleruvkc) = qofuihgs(hleruvkc) = 0$. We may thus take $vpsgnkmu = hleruvkc$ hereafter.

Since $\int_0^{vpsgnkmu} qofuihgs(zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt = -\int_0^{1-vpsgnkmu} qofuihgs(1-zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt$,
we may assume that $vpsgnkmu \leq 1/2$. By then substituting $-qofuihgs(zemyofqt)$
for $qofuihgs(zemyofqt)$ if needed, we may assume that $\int_0^{vpsgnkmu} qofuihgs(zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt \geq 0$.
{}From the inequality $qofuihgs'(zemyofqt) \geq -moypqksa$, we deduce
$qofuihgs(zemyofqt) \leq moypqksa(vpsgnkmu - zemyofqt)$ for $0 \leq zemyofqt \leq vpsgnkmu$, so
\begin{align*}
\int_0^{vpsgnkmu} qofuihgs(zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt \leq &\int_0^{vpsgnkmu} moypqksa(vpsgnkmu-zemyofqt)\,dzemyofqt \\
&= -\left. \frac{1}{2} moypqksa (vpsgnkmu - zemyofqt)^2 \right|_0^{vpsgnkmu} \\
&= \frac{vpsgnkmu^2}{2} moypqksa \leq \frac{1}{8} moypqksa
\end{align*}
as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
  f : [0,3] \longrightarrow \mathbb R  
be absolutely continuous and assume that its (a.e. defined) derivative satisfies
|f'(x)| \le M \qquad (0\le x\le 3) 
for some constant M>0.  Suppose further that
\[
\int_{0}^{3} f(x)\,dx = 0.
\]
Prove that for every \alpha\in[0,3]
\[
\Bigl|\int_{0}^{\alpha} f(x)\,dx\Bigr|\;\le\;\frac{9}{8}\,M.
\]
(The numerical constant 9/8 is sharp.)
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Notation and first observations.
Put
    g(x):=\int_{0}^{x}f(t)\,dt\qquad(0\le x\le 3),\qquad
    G:=\max_{0\le x\le 3}|g(x)|.
Because f is absolutely continuous,  g is continuously differentiable,
g(0)=g(3)=0 and g'(x)=f(x) for a.e. x.  If g\equiv 0 the desired
estimate is immediate; hence we assume G>0.  After replacing f by -f if
necessary we may suppose that g attains the value G (and therefore its
maximum) somewhere and that G>0.

2.  Reduction to the half-interval [0,3/2] by reflection.
If g reaches its positive maximum at a point x_{0}\le 3/2 we keep the
function as it is.  If x_{0}>3/2 we reflect the graph in the midpoint
x\mapsto3-x and work with
   \widetilde f(x):=f(3-x),\;\widetilde g(x):=\int_{0}^{x}\widetilde f.
The reflected function has the same bound on the derivative, satisfies
\widetilde g(0)=\widetilde g(3)=0 and still has maximum value G, this time
attained at y_{0}:=3-x_{0}\in(0,3/2].  Hence it suffices to treat the
case in which some maximiser lies in the interval [0,3/2].  From now on
we assume
          0<\alpha_{*}\le\frac32,\qquad g(\alpha_{*})=G.

3.  Approximating the maximiser by points where g' exists.
The derivative g'=f exists almost everywhere, so the exceptional set
N:=\{x\in[0,3]: g'(x) \text{ does not exist}\} has measure 0.  For each
\varepsilon>0 choose a point \alpha\in(0,3/2]\setminus N such that
      g(\alpha)>G-\varepsilon.              (1)
(This is possible because the complement of N is dense.)  Fix such an
\alpha and keep \varepsilon>0 arbitrary for the moment.

4.  An upper bound for |f(\alpha)|.
Let h>0 be so small that \alpha+h\le3.  Using the fundamental theorem of
calculus twice and the bound |f'|\le M we obtain
\begin{align*}
 g(\alpha+h)-g(\alpha)
      &=\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+h}f(t)\,dt\\
      &=f(\alpha)h+ \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+h}\!\int_{\alpha}^{t} f'(s)\,ds\,dt\\
      &=f(\alpha)h+\theta_{+},\qquad |\theta_{+}|\le \frac{M h^{2}}{2}.
\end{align*}
Because g(\alpha+h)\le G and g(\alpha)\ge G-\varepsilon,  we have
        f(\alpha)h \le \varepsilon + \frac{M h^{2}}{2}.             (2)
Repeating the same computation with a negative step -h (and \alpha-h\ge0)
we derive
       -f(\alpha)h \le \varepsilon + \frac{M h^{2}}{2}.             (3)
Together (2)-(3) give, for every 0<h\le\min\{\alpha,3-\alpha\},
        |f(\alpha)| \le \frac{\varepsilon}{h}+\frac{M h}{2}.          (4)
Choosing the optimal step  h:=\sqrt{2\varepsilon/M}  (which is admissible
when \varepsilon is small enough) we finally obtain
        |f(\alpha)| \le \sqrt{2M\varepsilon}.                       (5)

5.  Estimating g(\alpha).
Write
   g(\alpha)=\int_{0}^{\alpha}f(x)\,dx=
             \int_{0}^{\alpha}\bigl(f(x)-f(\alpha)\bigr)\,dx+f(\alpha)\alpha.
For the first integral we use |f(x)-f(\alpha)|\le M|x-\alpha|
(which follows from |f'|\le M) to get
   \Bigl|\int_{0}^{\alpha}\bigl(f(x)-f(\alpha)\bigr)dx\Bigr|
             \le M\!\int_{0}^{\alpha}|x-\alpha|dx=\frac{M\alpha^{2}}{2}.
Combining this with (5) yields
        g(\alpha)\le \frac{M\alpha^{2}}{2}+\alpha\sqrt{2M\varepsilon}.  (6)
Since \alpha\le3/2, letting \varepsilon\to0 in (6) gives
        g(\alpha)\le \frac{M\alpha^{2}}{2}\le \frac{M}{2}\Bigl(\frac32\Bigr)^{2}
                     =\frac{9}{8}M.

6.  Passage to the true maximum.
Inequality (6) holds for every \varepsilon>0 and every corresponding
point \alpha satisfying (1).  Taking the limit \varepsilon\to0 we obtain
G=\max_{[0,3]}g\le 9M/8.  Therefore
     \bigl|\,g(x)\,\bigr|\le G\le \frac{9}{8}M\qquad(0\le x\le3),
which is exactly the required estimate
     \Bigl|\int_{0}^{\alpha}f(x)\,dx\Bigr|\le\frac{9}{8}M\qquad(0\le\alpha\le3).

7.  Sharpness of the constant.
Equality is realised (up to scaling) by the piecewise quadratic function
whose second derivative alternates between \pm M on the three sub-intervals
[0,\tfrac32], [\tfrac32,\tfrac52] and [\tfrac52,3].  Hence 9/8 cannot be
improved.

This completes the proof.

core_steps: Define g(x)=∫₀ˣ f, note g(0)=g(1)=0 ⇒ |g| attains max at α with f(α)=0 (critical–point argument).
Use symmetry (g(α)=−g(1−α)) and sign–flip (replace f by −f if needed) to arrange α≤1/2 and ∫₀^α f≥0.
From |f'|≤B deduce f(x)=f(α)−∫ₓ^α f' ≥ −B(α−x) ⇒ f(x) ≤ B(α−x) on [0,α].
Integrate this linear bound: ∫₀^α f ≤ ∫₀^α B(α−x)dx = Bα²/2.
Combine α≤1/2 with previous line to get ∫₀^α |f| ≤ B/8, i.e. desired inequality.

slot1.description: Length-1 domain [0,1]; any fixed finite interval of length L would work after rescaling.

slot2.description: Numerical mid-point used for symmetry reduction; any c with 0<c<1 giving α≤c would only change the final constant.

slot3.description: Resulting constant in the inequality, coming from (α≤1/2)²/2; changes if slot2 changes or if interval length scales.

slot4.description: Assumption that f has a continuous derivative; bounded (or merely integrable) derivative suffices for the integral estimates.
